title: Encephalitis
oneLiner: Brain parenchymal inflammation, usually viral (especially HSV-1), presenting with fever, altered consciousness, and seizures; needs urgent acyclovir.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Bacterial meningitis** — *Neck stiffness + petechial/purpuric rash + rapid deterioration* → give IV ceftriaxone + dexamethasone before CT if no contraindication
- **Raised intracranial pressure (ICP)** — *Cushing's triad (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing) + pupillary asymmetry* → emergency neurosurgery consultation; mannitol/hypertonic saline if ICP crisis
- **Status epilepticus** — *Prolonged seizure >5 min or without return to baseline* → ABC, IV lorazepam, then phenytoin/levetiracetam; treat underlying encephalitis concurrently
sections.Presentation:
- Classic triad: fever + headache + altered mental state (confusion, behaviour change, reduced GCS)
- Seizures (focal or generalised) are common, often early
- Focal neurological deficits (e.g., aphasia, hemiparesis, visual field loss) — particularly temporal lobe signs in HSV
- Meningism (neck stiffness, photophobia) — suggests meningoencephalitis
- Prodromal viral illness (non-specific) may precede neurological phase
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Viral encephalitis** (HSV-1 most common) vs **bacterial meningitis** — meningism + rash + high CSF WCC with neutrophils favours bacterial
- **Brain abscess** — focal signs + mass effect on CT/MRI; slower onset; may have fever but not always
- **Stroke** — acute focal deficit without fever; CT/MRI shows infarction
- **Delirium / sepsis-associated encephalopathy** — no CSF pleocytosis; normal MRI; responds to treatment of underlying infection
- **Autoimmune encephalitis** (e.g., anti-NMDA receptor) — subacute onset, psychiatric symptoms, movement disorder, ovarian teratoma (in young women); CSF negative for viruses, positive for antibodies
- **Post-infectious encephalomyelitis (ADEM)** — acute demyelination after infection/vaccination; MRI shows multifocal white matter lesions

**Splitter:** CSF PCR for HSV-1/2 (positive = HSV encephalitis; negative with typical MRI features → consider autoimmune or other virus)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** capillary blood glucose (exclude hypoglycaemia), temperature, NEWS2, mental status assessment (AVPU/GCS)
- **Bloods:** FBC, CRP, U&E, LFT, clotting screen, blood cultures, HIV serology (perform in all), syphilis serology, autoantibody screen (if suspect autoimmune)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT head (non-contrast) – to exclude mass, bleed, or raised ICP before LP
  - **Second-line:** MRI brain with contrast – preferred for parenchymal changes (temporal lobe hyperintensity in HSV); can show ADEM, abscess, vasculitis
- **Special tests:**
  - **Lumbar puncture (LP)** – after CT if no contraindication. Measure opening pressure. CSF: protein, glucose, lactate, cell count + differential, Gram stain, bacterial culture, viral PCR (HSV-1/2, VZV, enterovirus), TB culture if risk factors. Consider autoantibody panel if viral negative.
  - EEG – helpful if seizures or non-convulsive status; may show periodic lateralising epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) in HSV
- **Diagnostic criteria:** IDSA/ESCMID guideline criteria for encephalitis: fever + altered consciousness + CSF pleocytosis (or MRI/EEG evidence of encephalitis) plus microbiological confirmation. For HSV: temporal lobe MRI changes + CSF PCR positive.
- **Never-miss test:** **Lumbar puncture** (after CT) – omit only if absolutely contraindicated (e.g., coagulopathy, signs of herniation); if deferred, start acyclovir empirically
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - ABCD assessment, airway support if GCS <8
  - Seizure control: IV lorazepam 4 mg (repeat once) or diazepam PR
  - **Start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg TDS** (do not wait for LP if delay >1 h)
  - IV ceftriaxone 2 g BD if bacterial meningitis not excluded (until LP results)
  - Manage raised ICP: nurse head up 30°, avoid hypercapnia, consider mannitol 0.25–0.5 g/kg IV if CT shows oedema
- **Ward (first 24-72 h):**
  - Continue acyclovir until CSF HSV PCR negative (typically 14–21 days for confirmed HSV)
  - Neuro observations Q1–2h (GCS, pupils, limb power)
  - Seizure prophylaxis? Not routinely; treat active seizures only
  - Hyponatraemia monitoring – SIADH common; fluid restrict if symptomatic, avoid overcorrection
  - Consider IV steroids (dexamethasone) if brain oedema or if suspect autoimmune (but not in viral unless severe oedema)
  - If autoimmune confirmed: high-dose IV methylprednisolone, IVIG or plasma exchange per specialist
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Antiviral for 14–21 days (oral valacyclovir may complete course after IV if stable)
  - Neurorehabilitation: early referral for physical, speech, occupational therapy
  - Neuropsychiatric follow-up: cognitive impairment, mood disorders common
  - Review for post-encephalitic epilepsy – may need long-term AED
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance rate (e.g., 1 mL/kg/h) – avoid hypotonic fluids due to SIADH risk
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) if no active bleeding or coagulopathy
  - **Diet:** NBM initially if GCS <10 or swallow unsafe; otherwise normal
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 Q1h (first 24 h), then Q4h if stable; neuro obs (GCS, pupils) Q1h for first 72 h
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agent:** Aciclovir IV – **10 mg/kg IV TDS** (slow infusion over 1 hour). Duration: 14–21 days for HSV encephalitis.
- **Alternative antivirals:** Ganciclovir IV (for CMV); oseltamivir (if influenza-related encephalitis)
- **Key contraindications:** Caution in renal impairment – reduce dose when CrCl <25 mL/min ([check local renal dosing policy]). Avoid if known hypersensitivity.
- **Interactions:** Aciclovir can potentiate nephrotoxicity of other drugs (e.g., gentamicin, vancomycin). Probenecid reduces clearance.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Aciclovir dose must be adjusted for CrCl – [check local policy for exact reduction] (e.g., CrCl 10–25: 10 mg/kg IV Q12h). No hepatic adjustment needed.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving acyclovir without monitoring renal function** – acute kidney injury can occur; always check U&E and eGFR before each dose. Second error: **under-dosing in normal renal function** – 5 mg/kg is for mucocutaneous HSV, NOT encephalitis.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Raised ICP (leading to herniation), seizures (including non-convulsive status), SIADH/hyponatraemia, respiratory failure (if low GCS), stroke (from vasculitis or thrombosis), cerebral oedema, drug-induced AKI (aciclovir crystals)
- **Chronic:** Post-encephalitic epilepsy (up to 20%), cognitive deficits (memory, executive function), behavioural changes, chronic headache, fatigue, depression, persistent focal deficits (e.g., aphasia)
sections.Prognosis: - HSV encephalitis: mortality ~70% without treatment, <20% with acyclovir. Of survivors, >50% have significant neurological sequelae. Other viral encephalitis (enterovirus, VZV) generally better prognosis. Autoimmune encephalitis often reversible with immunotherapy but may relapse.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Start acyclovir immediately** if clinical suspicion of encephalitis – do not wait for LP or imaging results (can delay)
- **Always check HIV and syphilis serology** in all encephalitis patients – treatable causes
- **Consider autoimmune encephalitis** in young women with psychiatric onset and no fever – send CSF for antibody panel (NMDA, LGI1, etc.)
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Delaying acyclovir for LP** – if LP cannot be done within 1 hour, start empiric therapy
- **Assuming negative CSF PCR rules out encephalitis** – false negatives occur early; repeat LP at 48–72 h if high suspicion
- **Giving corticosteroids to viral encephalitis without excluding bacterial infection** – can worsen fungal/TB; use only under specialist guidance (e.g., severe oedema or autoimmune)
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** new headache, worsening confusion or behaviour change, any seizure, focal weakness, visual disturbance, fever recurrence
- **Follow-up:** Outpatient neurology clinic within 2 weeks; neuropsychiatric assessment if cognitive/behavioural issues; repeat MRI if clinically indicated
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU:** GCS drop >2 points, new focal signs, seizures (especially >1 in 2 h), NEWS2 score ≥5, signs of raised ICP (Cushing's, pupil asymmetry), rapid deterioration in consciousness
- **Specific thresholds:** GCS ≤8 → consider ICU for airway protection; lactate >4 mmol/L suggests sepsis; creatinine rise >50% from baseline (consider acyclovir nephrotoxicity)

rawMarkdown:
## Encephalitis

**One-liner:** Brain parenchymal inflammation, usually viral (especially HSV-1), presenting with fever, altered consciousness, and seizures; needs urgent acyclovir.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Bacterial meningitis** — *Neck stiffness + petechial/purpuric rash + rapid deterioration* → give IV ceftriaxone + dexamethasone before CT if no contraindication
- **Raised intracranial pressure (ICP)** — *Cushing's triad (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing) + pupillary asymmetry* → emergency neurosurgery consultation; mannitol/hypertonic saline if ICP crisis
- **Status epilepticus** — *Prolonged seizure >5 min or without return to baseline* → ABC, IV lorazepam, then phenytoin/levetiracetam; treat underlying encephalitis concurrently

### Presentation
- Classic triad: fever + headache + altered mental state (confusion, behaviour change, reduced GCS)
- Seizures (focal or generalised) are common, often early
- Focal neurological deficits (e.g., aphasia, hemiparesis, visual field loss) — particularly temporal lobe signs in HSV
- Meningism (neck stiffness, photophobia) — suggests meningoencephalitis
- Prodromal viral illness (non-specific) may precede neurological phase

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Viral encephalitis** (HSV-1 most common) vs **bacterial meningitis** — meningism + rash + high CSF WCC with neutrophils favours bacterial
- **Brain abscess** — focal signs + mass effect on CT/MRI; slower onset; may have fever but not always
- **Stroke** — acute focal deficit without fever; CT/MRI shows infarction
- **Delirium / sepsis-associated encephalopathy** — no CSF pleocytosis; normal MRI; responds to treatment of underlying infection
- **Autoimmune encephalitis** (e.g., anti-NMDA receptor) — subacute onset, psychiatric symptoms, movement disorder, ovarian teratoma (in young women); CSF negative for viruses, positive for antibodies
- **Post-infectious encephalomyelitis (ADEM)** — acute demyelination after infection/vaccination; MRI shows multifocal white matter lesions

**Splitter:** CSF PCR for HSV-1/2 (positive = HSV encephalitis; negative with typical MRI features → consider autoimmune or other virus)

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** capillary blood glucose (exclude hypoglycaemia), temperature, NEWS2, mental status assessment (AVPU/GCS)
- **Bloods:** FBC, CRP, U&E, LFT, clotting screen, blood cultures, HIV serology (perform in all), syphilis serology, autoantibody screen (if suspect autoimmune)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT head (non-contrast) – to exclude mass, bleed, or raised ICP before LP
  - **Second-line:** MRI brain with contrast – preferred for parenchymal changes (temporal lobe hyperintensity in HSV); can show ADEM, abscess, vasculitis
- **Special tests:**
  - **Lumbar puncture (LP)** – after CT if no contraindication. Measure opening pressure. CSF: protein, glucose, lactate, cell count + differential, Gram stain, bacterial culture, viral PCR (HSV-1/2, VZV, enterovirus), TB culture if risk factors. Consider autoantibody panel if viral negative.
  - EEG – helpful if seizures or non-convulsive status; may show periodic lateralising epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) in HSV
- **Diagnostic criteria:** IDSA/ESCMID guideline criteria for encephalitis: fever + altered consciousness + CSF pleocytosis (or MRI/EEG evidence of encephalitis) plus microbiological confirmation. For HSV: temporal lobe MRI changes + CSF PCR positive.
- **Never-miss test:** **Lumbar puncture** (after CT) – omit only if absolutely contraindicated (e.g., coagulopathy, signs of herniation); if deferred, start acyclovir empirically

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - ABCD assessment, airway support if GCS <8
  - Seizure control: IV lorazepam 4 mg (repeat once) or diazepam PR
  - **Start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg TDS** (do not wait for LP if delay >1 h)
  - IV ceftriaxone 2 g BD if bacterial meningitis not excluded (until LP results)
  - Manage raised ICP: nurse head up 30°, avoid hypercapnia, consider mannitol 0.25–0.5 g/kg IV if CT shows oedema
- **Ward (first 24-72 h):**
  - Continue acyclovir until CSF HSV PCR negative (typically 14–21 days for confirmed HSV)
  - Neuro observations Q1–2h (GCS, pupils, limb power)
  - Seizure prophylaxis? Not routinely; treat active seizures only
  - Hyponatraemia monitoring – SIADH common; fluid restrict if symptomatic, avoid overcorrection
  - Consider IV steroids (dexamethasone) if brain oedema or if suspect autoimmune (but not in viral unless severe oedema)
  - If autoimmune confirmed: high-dose IV methylprednisolone, IVIG or plasma exchange per specialist
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Antiviral for 14–21 days (oral valacyclovir may complete course after IV if stable)
  - Neurorehabilitation: early referral for physical, speech, occupational therapy
  - Neuropsychiatric follow-up: cognitive impairment, mood disorders common
  - Review for post-encephalitic epilepsy – may need long-term AED
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance rate (e.g., 1 mL/kg/h) – avoid hypotonic fluids due to SIADH risk
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) if no active bleeding or coagulopathy
  - **Diet:** NBM initially if GCS <10 or swallow unsafe; otherwise normal
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 Q1h (first 24 h), then Q4h if stable; neuro obs (GCS, pupils) Q1h for first 72 h

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agent:** Aciclovir IV – **10 mg/kg IV TDS** (slow infusion over 1 hour). Duration: 14–21 days for HSV encephalitis.
- **Alternative antivirals:** Ganciclovir IV (for CMV); oseltamivir (if influenza-related encephalitis)
- **Key contraindications:** Caution in renal impairment – reduce dose when CrCl <25 mL/min ([check local renal dosing policy]). Avoid if known hypersensitivity.
- **Interactions:** Aciclovir can potentiate nephrotoxicity of other drugs (e.g., gentamicin, vancomycin). Probenecid reduces clearance.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Aciclovir dose must be adjusted for CrCl – [check local policy for exact reduction] (e.g., CrCl 10–25: 10 mg/kg IV Q12h). No hepatic adjustment needed.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving acyclovir without monitoring renal function** – acute kidney injury can occur; always check U&E and eGFR before each dose. Second error: **under-dosing in normal renal function** – 5 mg/kg is for mucocutaneous HSV, NOT encephalitis.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Raised ICP (leading to herniation), seizures (including non-convulsive status), SIADH/hyponatraemia, respiratory failure (if low GCS), stroke (from vasculitis or thrombosis), cerebral oedema, drug-induced AKI (aciclovir crystals)
- **Chronic:** Post-encephalitic epilepsy (up to 20%), cognitive deficits (memory, executive function), behavioural changes, chronic headache, fatigue, depression, persistent focal deficits (e.g., aphasia)

### Prognosis
- HSV encephalitis: mortality ~70% without treatment, <20% with acyclovir. Of survivors, >50% have significant neurological sequelae. Other viral encephalitis (enterovirus, VZV) generally better prognosis. Autoimmune encephalitis often reversible with immunotherapy but may relapse.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Start acyclovir immediately** if clinical suspicion of encephalitis – do not wait for LP or imaging results (can delay)
- **Always check HIV and syphilis serology** in all encephalitis patients – treatable causes
- **Consider autoimmune encephalitis** in young women with psychiatric onset and no fever – send CSF for antibody panel (NMDA, LGI1, etc.)

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Delaying acyclovir for LP** – if LP cannot be done within 1 hour, start empiric therapy
- **Assuming negative CSF PCR rules out encephalitis** – false negatives occur early; repeat LP at 48–72 h if high suspicion
- **Giving corticosteroids to viral encephalitis without excluding bacterial infection** – can worsen fungal/TB; use only under specialist guidance (e.g., severe oedema or autoimmune)

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** new headache, worsening confusion or behaviour change, any seizure, focal weakness, visual disturbance, fever recurrence
- **Follow-up:** Outpatient neurology clinic within 2 weeks; neuropsychiatric assessment if cognitive/behavioural issues; repeat MRI if clinically indicated

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU:** GCS drop >2 points, new focal signs, seizures (especially >1 in 2 h), NEWS2 score ≥5, signs of raised ICP (Cushing's, pupil asymmetry), rapid deterioration in consciousness
- **Specific thresholds:** GCS ≤8 → consider ICU for airway protection; lactate >4 mmol/L suggests sepsis; creatinine rise >50% from baseline (consider acyclovir nephrotoxicity)
